1: 'Wives, in the same way submit yourselves to your own husbands so that, if any of them do not believe the word, they may be won over without words by the behavior of their wives' — the 'in the same way' (homoiōs) connects the wives' submission to the Christ-pattern of 1 Peter 2:21-25 and the slaves' submission of 1 Peter 2:18-20; the household code throughout is grounded in the Christ-example. The specific scenario — wives of non-believing husbands — was a common pastoral situation: the Pauline letters also address it (1 Corinthians 7:12-16). The argument is missionary-pragmatic: conduct speaks louder than words to a husband who has rejected the verbal proclamation.
2: 'When they see the purity and reverence of your lives' — the 'purity' (hagnēn) is chastity and marital faithfulness; the 'reverence' (phobō) is the fear of God that governs the wife's conduct. The observation that conduct is 'seen' — that character is visible — presupposes the intimate social context of the household, where the wife's daily behavior is directly observed by her husband. The strategy of silent witness was not unique to Peter: Stoic moral philosophers also argued that the virtuous person's life is itself a philosophical demonstration.
3: 'Your beauty should not come from outward adornment, such as elaborate hairstyles and the wearing of gold jewelry and fine clothes' — the critique of ostentatious jewelry and hairstyles was a standard topos in Greco-Roman moral philosophy (Plutarch, Musonius Rufus, Seneca all warn against excessive female adornment as evidence of vanity and moral weakness) and in Hellenistic Jewish literature (Philo). The specific items — braided hair, gold jewelry, fine clothing — are the markers of Roman aristocratic female display; Peter's critique aligns Christian women's conduct with the counter-cultural critique of luxury that both Jewish and Stoic moralists shared.
4: 'Rather, it should be that of your inner self, the unfading beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is of great worth in God's sight' — the 'inner self' (ho kryptos tēs kardias anthrōpos, the hidden person of the heart) mirrors Paul's 'inner person' (ho esō anthrōpos, Romans 7:22; 2 Corinthians 4:16; Ephesians 3:16). The 'gentle and quiet spirit' (pneumatos praeōs kai hēsychiou) is the character quality that the Beatitudes associate with the kingdom (Matthew 5:5: blessed are the meek). The assessment 'of great worth in God's sight' (enōpion tou theou polyteles) redirects the honor-economy: divine valuation of inner character over human valuation of external display.
5: 'For this is the way the holy women of the past who put their hope in God used to adorn themselves. They submitted themselves to their own husbands' — the 'holy women of the past' (hai hagiai gynaikes hai ... kosmousai heautas) are the matriarchs — Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Leah — who are commended in Jewish tradition as models of virtue. The matriarchs are the female counterparts to the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; citing them grounds Peter's instruction in the long covenantal tradition of Israel's founding families.
6: 'Like Sarah, who obeyed Abraham and called him her lord. You are her daughters if you do what is right and do not give way to fear' — the citation of Sarah calling Abraham 'lord' (kyrios) refers to Genesis 18:12 LXX (Sarah's inner thought that Abraham is 'my lord'). The 'daughters of Sarah' (tekna Saras) are those who inherit her posture — just as Paul's 'children of Abraham' (Galatians 3:7) are those who share Abraham's faith. The prohibition 'do not give way to fear' is the pastoral assurance: the submission Peter calls for is not coerced by terror but chosen in freedom and hope.
7: 'Husbands, in the same way be considerate as you live with your wives, and treat them with respect as the weaker partner and as heirs together of the gracious gift of life' — the 'weaker partner' (asthenesteri skeuei, the weaker vessel) is a social-physical assessment in the context of Greco-Roman medical anthropology, not a judgment of moral or spiritual inferiority. The theological counterweight: 'heirs together of the gracious gift of life' (synklēronomois charitos zōēs) — in the eschatological inheritance, husband and wife are co-heirs with equal standing. The eschatological equality relativizes the hierarchical household code without abolishing its structure in the present age.
8: 'Finally, all of you, be like-minded, be sympathetic, love one another, be compassionate and humble' — the five virtues of the community summary: homophrones (like-minded, sharing one mind), sympatheis (sympathetic, suffering with), philadelphoi (brother-loving), eusplanchnoi (compassionate, rich in inner feeling), tapeinophrones (humble-minded). These five are the character marks that the gospel produces in a diaspora community that must maintain cohesion under social pressure. The 'humble-minded' closes the list in contrast to the alazoneia (arrogant boasting) of Greco-Roman competitive culture.
9: 'Do not repay evil with evil or insult with insult. On the contrary, repay with blessing, because to this you were called so that you may inherit a blessing' — the non-retaliation ethic mirrors the Christ-pattern of 2:23 and the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:38-42: do not resist an evildoer; 1 Peter 5:11-12: blessed are you when people insult you). The calling to 'inherit a blessing' by blessing is the eschatological logic: the covenant blessing (Genesis 12:2-3: I will bless you and you will be a blessing) is inherited by those who embody the blessing-over-curse pattern rather than retaliating within the honor-shame competition.
10: 'Whoever would love life and see good days must keep their tongue from evil and their lips from deceitful speech' — Peter begins a long quotation from Psalms 34:12-16, which runs through 1 Peter 3:12. Psalm 34 (an acrostic of David's deliverance from Abimelech, probably Achish of Gath, 1 Samuel 21:10-15) is a wisdom psalm commending the fear of the LORD as the path to life and blessing. Peter uses the Psalm as the scriptural warrant for his community ethics of speech and peacemaking under social pressure.
11: 'They must turn from evil and do good; they must seek peace and pursue it' — continuing the Psalms 34:14 citation. The peace-seeking imperative (zētēsatō eirēnēn kai diōxatō autēn) is intensified by the verb 'pursue' (diōkō, to chase, to hunt) — peace is not passive but actively hunted. The irony that a community being hunted by social hostility is called to actively hunt peace is embedded in Peter's appropriation of Ps 34: the same Psalm that describes the LORD's protection of the righteous begins with the exhortation to flee from evil and pursue peace.
12: 'For the eyes of the Lord are on the righteous and his ears are attentive to their prayer, but the face of the Lord is against those who do evil' — Psalms 34:15-16 provides the theological foundation for the non-retaliation ethic: the LORD himself attends to the righteous and opposes evildoers. The community does not need to defend itself because the LORD's eyes and ears are already turned toward them, and the LORD's face (the most threatening expression of divine power) is already turned against their persecutors. The eschatological vindication is not the community's to achieve — it belongs to the Judge.
13: 'Who is going to harm you if you are eager to do good?' — the rhetorical question assumes that the normal Roman judicial system will not target genuinely good citizens. Roman legal culture valued civic virtue and good order; a community that is visibly contributing to social goods rather than threatening them has practical legal protection. The argument is not naively optimistic (Peter knows his communities face hostility) but strategic: the first line of defense against false accusation is genuine virtue that makes the accusation implausible.
14: 'But even if you should suffer for what is right, you are blessed. 'Do not fear what they fear; do not be frightened'' — the beatitude ('you are blessed,' makarioi) echoes Matthew 5:10-12 (blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness). The quotation is from Isaiah 8:12-13 LXX, where Isaiah instructs his disciples not to fear the conspiracy that Ahaz fears (the Syro-Ephraimite coalition), but to fear the LORD alone. Peter applies the Isaianic call to fearlessness in the face of political threat to his communities' situation under Roman social pressure.
15: 'But in your hearts revere Christ as Lord. Always be prepared to give an answer to everyone who asks you to give the reason for the hope that you have' — the 'apologetics' instruction uses the technical legal term apologia (defense, formal answer to a charge) — the same word Paul uses in Philippians 1:7 and 2 Timothy 4:16 for his legal defense before Roman courts. The community is to be ready to give a formal verbal account of their hope; the context suggests formal interrogation by Roman authorities rather than mere casual conversation. The setting is the kind of investigation that Pliny the Younger conducted with Christians in Bithynia (10.96).
16: 'Keeping a clear conscience, so that those who speak maliciously against your good behavior in Christ may be ashamed of their slander' — the 'clear conscience' (syneidesin echousin agathēn) is a legal-forensic concept: the conscience that has no hidden guilt, so that a formal examination produces vindication rather than shame. The 'shame' of the accusers when their false charges cannot be sustained is the reversal of the honor-shame dynamics: those who slandered the community discover that the community's conduct disproves their accusations, and the shame returns to the accusers.
17: 'For it is better, if it is God's will, to suffer for doing good than for doing evil' — the comparative 'it is better' (kreitton) applies the conventional Greek moral calculus of better-worse to a non-conventional conclusion. Greek wisdom (Aristotle, Socrates) agreed that it is better to suffer injustice than to do it; Peter takes this further — it is better to suffer unjustly (specifically as a Christian doing good) than to suffer justly (as an evildoer who deserves punishment). The grounding is theological: God's will (to thelēma tou theou) governs the community's suffering — it is not random.
18: 'For Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God. He was put to death in the body but made alive in the Spirit' — the 'once for sins' (hapax peri hamartiōn) echoes the language of Hebrews (1 Peter 7:27; 1 Peter 9:12, 26, 28; 1 Peter 10:10) for the once-for-all definitive nature of Christ's atoning death. The 'righteous for the unrighteous' (dikaios hyper adikōn) is the substitutionary formula. The death-in-body / alive-in-spirit contrast frames the passage into the difficult 1 Peter 3:19-20: the spiritual realm in which Christ operated 'after being made alive' is the domain of the proclamation to the imprisoned spirits.
19: 'After being made alive, he went and made proclamation to the imprisoned spirits' — the most debated verse in 1 Peter. The 'imprisoned spirits' (pneumasin en phylakē) are most likely the Watchers — the fallen divine beings of Genesis 6:1-4 who, in the 1 Enoch tradition (chapters 6-16), were imprisoned in Sheol awaiting judgment. Peter draws on this tradition (2 Peter 2:4-5 and Jude 6 also reference the imprisoned angels) to describe Christ's victory-proclamation to the forces that had corrupted pre-flood humanity. The proclamation is not evangelism but a herald's announcement of Christ's triumph over cosmic powers.
20: 'To those who were disobedient long ago when God waited patiently in the days of Noah while the ark was being built' — the connection to Noah identifies the imprisoned spirits as those associated with the pre-flood era. God's patient waiting (apexedecheto... makrothymia) during Noah's ark-building is the model for the patience with which God waits for human response in Peter's own day (2 Peter 3:9: he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish). The 'eight persons' (Noah and his family) who were saved through water provide the typological connection to baptism in 1 Peter 3:21.
21: 'And this water symbolizes baptism that now saves you also — not the removal of dirt from the body but the pledge of a clear conscience toward God. It saves you by the resurrection of Jesus Christ' — the Noah-ark-water becomes the type (antitypon) of baptism: as eight were saved through water while the old world was destroyed, so believers are saved through baptismal water that marks the boundary between old and new creation. The clarification 'not the removal of dirt from the body' distinguishes Christian baptism from the ritual washings (ablutions) of Second Temple Judaism and Greco-Roman mystery religions; the efficacy is not the water itself but the resurrection of Christ and the conscience-pledge that accompanies it.
22: 'Who has gone into heaven and is at God's right hand — with angels, authorities and powers in submission to him' — the ascension and enthronement formula: Christ at the right hand of God (Psalms 110:1 LXX: sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool) is the controlling image of early Christian eschatology. The 'angels, authorities, and powers in submission to him' (angelōn kai exousiōn kai dynameōn hypotagerntōn autō) echoes the cosmic powers of Ephesians 1:21 and Colossians 2:15. The same Christ who proclaimed victory to the imprisoned spirits (1 Peter 3:19) is now enthroned above all cosmic powers — the entire angelic hierarchy is subject to him.
